title: Evenings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Dingell questions U-M's Los Alamos-linked data center amid ratepayer alarms over bills and resources.

lines.text: Good evening and welcome to Evenings with Graham & Emmy, the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
Tonight we're digging into a fresh congressional probe that ties the University of Michigan to a proposed hyperscale data center with Los Alamos connections.
That's the one raising big questions about power demand and water use right here in Michigan.
Rep. Debbie Dingell released letters today from U-M and Los Alamos leaders after she raised concerns about the project.
Why is a member of Congress getting involved in what sounds like a local siting fight?
Dingell is highlighting local worries over how much electricity and water a facility like this would pull, plus the university's role in backing it.
So what's the bigger picture here for regular folks near potential sites?
Residents could see higher utility bills and strained infrastructure if the project moves forward, according to the concerns laid out in the letters.
We've seen this pattern before with big power users. How does a data center actually drive up costs for everyone else?

These facilities need massive amounts of electricity around the clock and water for cooling. When demand spikes, utilities often build new plants or lines, and those costs get passed along through rate cases at the Public Service Commission.
And the water side? Michigan has plenty, but local wells and ponds matter too.
Exactly. In places like Saline, construction dewatering has already dropped nearby ponds by about two feet and lowered groundwater four to five feet. That's hitting farms and homes directly.
What about the university angle? Why would U-M be tied to something with Los Alamos?

The letters show Dingell is asking for details on any research or partnership links. Universities sometimes get involved in tech infrastructure, but residents want to know if public institutions are steering projects that could affect local resources.
Has this kind of pushback worked elsewhere in the state?
Yesterday Houghton put a one-year moratorium on new data centers after residents pushed back over power shortages. Neighboring Upper Peninsula townships are now looking at similar pauses.
So the Dingell probe could add momentum to those local efforts?

It keeps the spotlight on oversight and who pays the tab. The exchange is out there now, and it lines up with rallies planned for July 18 across more than fifty communities worried about AI facilities draining power and water.
That rally sounds like it's turning into a statewide movement.
Kent County activists are leading one in the Grand Rapids area, and the message is consistent: more oversight before these projects lock in big grid upgrades.
What happens next with the U-M project specifically?
Dingell is seeking more answers, so we could see follow-up letters or hearings. In the meantime, several southeast Michigan sites are still under review, including proposals in Mason and Taylor.
Mason has its own drama with accusations of fake grassroots support.
Yes, Ingham County neighbors say outside groups are manufacturing letters of support while locals worry about noise, power strain, and lost farmland.
That kind of trust issue can stall projects fast.
It already is. Officials are weighing next steps amid the fractured debate.
Over in Monroe, a utility analyst just warned officials about bill spikes.
Right. The expert told city leaders that unchecked growth means new power plants and transmission upgrades, with costs flowing to DTE and Consumers Energy customers.
Taylor residents are trying to write their own rules to block one.
They pitched a citizen ordinance adding zoning hurdles that could stop or reshape a proposed facility to protect property values and keep industrial-scale use out.
And Meta just cleared early hurdles for a giant campus in Wyoming.
That's a 715,000-square-foot facility in Kent County. It will pull substantial electricity, and neighbors are already asking what it means for their bills and local tax rolls.
Saline has the groundwater questions too.
Township officials are monitoring after construction dropped a resident pond roughly two feet. The contractor added water without prior notice, and summer checks continue.
So across the state the same themes keep coming up: power, water, and who decides.
That's why the Dingell letters land at a moment when more communities are stepping in with their own rules.
What should listeners watch for in the coming days?
The Michigan Public Service Commission meets today on large-load electric agreements. That directly affects how these data centers get hooked up to the grid.
And later this week?

Lansing City Council has a public hearing Monday on a 182-day data center moratorium. Saline Township trustees vote Tuesday on a 12-year tax abatement for the Oracle America Cloud Services project tied to the Related Digital site, plus a hearing on the Industrial Facilities Exemption Certificate.
Those are the meetings where residents can actually show up and speak.
Exactly. Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker. We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Dingell Probes U-M Data Center Plan as Ratepayers Face Power and Water Risks
